skill.name: finishing-a-development-branch
skill.description: Guides agents through the final steps after implementation: verify tests, choose a branch completion path, merge or create a PR, and clean up safely.
skill.summary: Finish completed development branches with structured test, merge, PR, and cleanup steps.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command detections are mostly true positives because the skill documents git, test, and GitHub CLI commands. The intent is legitimate branch completion, but merge, push, deletion, and worktree removal commands can change or delete local work. The weak cryptography and device-file detections are false positives with no malicious evidence found.

medium_findings.title: Operational Git Command Execution
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The skill instructs agents to run tests and git commands. This is legitimate for branch completion, but it changes repositories and remotes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit Markdown examples for tests, merge, push, and PR creation. No obfuscation or exfiltration is present, but these commands can alter local and remote repository state.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Branch and Worktree Cleanup
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. The discard path includes force branch deletion and worktree removal. The skill requires typed confirmation, which reduces but does not remove risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The destructive commands are clearly documented and can delete local branch or worktree data. The surrounding instructions add confirmation and scope limits, so malicious intent is not confirmed.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detection Is False Positive
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The flagged description line contains no cryptographic algorithm, crypto library, key handling, or encryption behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is plain skill metadata describing branch completion. I found no evidence of weak cryptographic code or intent in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Device File Detection Is Benign
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The /dev/null reference only suppresses stderr while checking git merge bases. It does not read secrets or write user data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The device file appears only in a standard shell redirection pattern. The surrounding command is branch-base detection, not filesystem scanning or data removal.

low_findings.title: Shell Substitution Used for Formatting
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. Command substitution is used to pass a heredoc PR body and to identify the current branch for worktree lookup.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The substitutions are visible and limited to local formatting or git state lookup. I found no evidence that untrusted input is executed as shell code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Force Delete Branch Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The discard workflow includes git branch -D, which can delete a local branch even when it is unmerged.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The force deletion command is explicitly shown. Its risk is operational data loss, not confirmed malware.

dangerous_patterns.title: Worktree Removal Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The cleanup workflow includes git worktree remove, which can remove a checked-out worktree path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The removal command is directly present in the workflow. The skill limits when it should run, but the command remains destructive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Push and PR Creation
dangerous_patterns.description: The PR workflow includes git push and GitHub CLI PR creation, which can publish branch contents to a remote.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The remote publishing commands are explicit. They support the stated purpose, but users should review branch contents before running them.

content.value_statement: Completed branches often need careful merge, PR, or cleanup decisions. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured finish workflow.
actual_capabilities: Verifies project tests before presenting completion choices.
Finds or confirms the base branch for integration.
Presents four clear options: merge, create PR, keep branch, or discard.
Defines merge, push, PR creation, and cleanup command sequences.
Requires typed confirmation before discard operations.
Documents when to preserve or remove a git worktree.
limitations: It does not detect every project-specific test command automatically.
It assumes git and GitHub CLI commands are available when needed.
It cannot guarantee remote branch policies or CI rules will pass.
It can delete local branches or worktrees if discard is confirmed.

use_cases.title: Finish a Feature Branch
use_cases.description: A developer wants a clear path after tests pass and implementation is complete.

use_cases.title: Create a Pull Request
use_cases.description: A maintainer wants an agent to push a branch and prepare a concise PR description.

use_cases.title: Clean Up Worktrees
use_cases.description: A team using git worktrees needs consistent cleanup after merge or discard decisions.

prompt_templates.title: Finish My Branch
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to finish the current development branch. Verify tests, then show me the available completion options.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow after implementation is complete.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a PR
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to verify the branch and guide me through pushing it and creating a pull request.
prompt_templates.scenario: Standard PR creation for a completed branch.

prompt_templates.title: Merge Locally
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to verify tests, identify the base branch, merge locally, rerun tests, and clean up when safe.
prompt_templates.scenario: Local integration before returning to the base branch.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Before Discard
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to evaluate whether this branch can be discarded. Show the commits and require confirmation before deleting anything.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced cleanup where local work may be deleted.

output_examples.input: Implementation is complete and tests pass. What should happen next?
output_examples.output: The assistant reports test status, identifies the base branch, and offers merge, PR, keep, or discard options.

output_examples.input: I want to discard this branch.
output_examples.output: The assistant lists the branch, commits, and worktree path, then waits for exact typed confirmation before deleting anything.

output_examples.input: Create a PR for this completed branch.
output_examples.output: The assistant pushes the branch when approved and prepares a PR with summary and test plan sections.

best_practices: Run the real project test command before choosing merge or PR actions.
Review branch contents before any push, merge, discard, or worktree removal.
Use typed confirmation for destructive cleanup and keep the confirmation text exact.
anti_patterns: Do not merge or create a PR when tests are failing.
Do not delete a branch or worktree without reviewing the listed commits.
Do not force-push unless the user explicitly requests that action.

faq.question: What does this skill help finish?
faq.answer: It helps finish completed development branches after implementation and tests are done.

faq.question: Does it run tests?
faq.answer: It instructs the agent to run the project test suite before offering completion options.

faq.question: Can it create pull requests?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a workflow for pushing a branch and creating a GitHub pull request.

faq.question: Can it delete work?
faq.answer: Yes. The discard workflow can delete local branches and worktrees after exact confirmation.

faq.question: Does it decide the base branch automatically?
faq.answer: It tries common base branches, then asks the user to confirm when needed.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
